channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Magic: The Gathering Marvel Super Heroes New Cards Revealed
item.content:encoded:
Today, Wizards of the Coast release a slew of new cards coming out for the Magic: The Gathering Marvel Super Heroes set! Releasing June 26th, the set brings home iconic, familiar heroes and villains. And we got a chance to check out a sneak peek last week, now we're collecting the best new cards revealed today for you to check out! Assemble!

First up, we have a full swath of new cards coming to the set, in their basic treatment (we'll spend some time with fancier cards in a moment). I'm especially excited from the batch of cards above to drop Mjölnir on my enemies. I love how thematic it feels that it deals 4 damage when it enters, and that cheap equip cost may feel restrictive in limited, but can be easily built around in Commander. The fact that you can pitch it to shock the board is icing on the Asgardian cake!

I'm also very interested in Leader, Super-Genius, and curious to see how this plays. Card draw is always good, obviously, and his built-in "target creature you control connives" ability ensures you don't have to craft fully around connive to get use out of him. Elektra is nasty and Monica Rambeau could be a great in the right commander deck. Hulk Smash and Madame Hydra both more a little more situational, though I bet Hulk Smash will find love in limited as two mana removal.

Standouts among the above cards include Thanos, in the press briefing Mark Rosewater described the challenge of depicting the six infinity stones when MTG only has five colors, but then remarked (confirming what many people speculated about the Infinity Stone cards) that there is technically a sixth color: colorless! 

Scarlett Witch is also completely bonkers, she'll be finding a happy home in my Prismari Storm commander deck real quick! And Arc Reactor is going to find many happy homes in artifact decks, where you could potentially cast it for very, very little mana with its Improvise ability. I wish Ten Rings didn't cost 8, but we can't get everything we want in this life.

Finally, for the batch of standard frame cards, we see Tony Stark and his flip-side. I think the Tony Stark side will fit really nicely into a Jhoira, Weatherlight Captain commander deck. We've already seen World War Hulk, but hot dawg that's a busted card. cEDH players are already eyeing it for the new favorite turbo deck featuring Raph & Mikey, Troublemakers.

Add to this batch the absolute goofiness of Worlds Without End, Thor's noncreature ping ability, and Thunderbolts Conspiracy as a must for build-around Villain decks, and we're beginning to see the shape and power level coming at us from Marvel Super Heroes. I think this is going to be a strong set, despite some murmurings that it'll feel like another Spider-man set, which had some shining stars but overall fell flat for many players.

Marvel Super Heroes Commander Decks

Up next, let's check out some key cards for four different Commander Decks coming out for Marvel Super Heroes.

The Avengers Assemble commander deck will feature Captain America, Team Leader as your headline commander, with Nick Fury as a solid backup option. Designed to hit hard with Heroes, I like the low casting cost of Captain America, as he'll likely be targeted often by your opponents.

Nick Fury gives you a different path to success, letting you do a little light digging for heroes while reducing their cost. From there, we see Captain Marvel, who's a bit expensive but has a nice buffing up ability that combos well with Cap. And Iron Man shows us that the focus is very firmly sitting with +1/+1 counters for this deck.

Doom Prevails focuses on the connive mechanic, allowing villains to connive and punishing opponents when you discard lands. Could we see some synergy with cycle lands? Loki's the alt-Commander here, letting you create illusions to punch through extra damage and draw cards. Fun, but very different, and I wonder if there will be enough support throughout the deck to run Loki instead of Doom.

Kang Prime turning nonland cards into cards with suspend could lead to some explosive turns, and Lady Loki's rules text may make your head spin for a moment, but the ability to ping and cast cards for free is not to be ignored!

The Fantastic Four commander Deck, led by Mister Fantastic, focuses on triggered abilities, as do the rest of the team. I worry a bit about the mana required to get this deck off the ground, but Invisible Woman's ability to make walls and make creatures unblockable is super thematic!

Human Torch gets to come out swinging and blasting opponents if you've cast a noncreature spell this turn, and The Thing gets stronger the more you cast noncreatures. It's fun that each has a very similar mechanic, but until we see some of the noncreature cards included in this deck it'll be hard to tell if the juice is worth the squeeze on this one.

Finally for the Commander Decks, we have Wakanda Forever! I love the idea of a Green/White deck that focuses on artifacts, and T'Challa wants to be spending those virbanium tokens on stuff like the Heart-Shaped Herb, which brings back the monarch mechanic (very thematic!) along with Okoye.

Okoye looks like a truly fun card to build around, gaining the monarch status before capitalizing off of losing it to another player. And Shuri fits in nicely here with her abilities gaining you serious benefits if you have enough artifacts on the board.

Marvel Super Heroes Borderless Panel Cards

We also got a look at some cool Borderless Panel Cards, and it's nice to see that these aren't all Sagas. We've seen these cards above already in their normal frame forms, so I'll just leave you to bask in their glory. Personally, I love the dynamic look of these, and the way they really evoke that comic book feel.

Marvel Super Heroes Borderless Source Material Cards

We're also getting fun Borderless Source Material cards, which bring back classics into the fold in limited quantities. Of all of these, I'm most interested in Show and Tell, which will truly hit the battlefield hard. And the look of these cards is just truly big, bold, and dynamic. Exactly what we want for the triumphant return of cards like Defense of the Heart.

Marvel Super Heroes Iconic Covers

Lastly, I'll leave you with what I think are just about the coolest cards to come out of this set - the Iconic Covers variants. With evocative, full-card art from these classic covers, these cards will absolutely turn heads when they hit the table!

What do you think of these new card reveals? Let us know in the comments below!

Giaco Furino joined the TechRaptor team as a Staff Writer in 2019 after searching for a dedicated place to write and talk about Tabletop Games. In 2020, he… More about Giaco



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/features/magic-gathering-marvel-super-heroes-new-cards-revealed
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Deep Dive Into Domains Of Dread With Ravenloft: The Horrors Within
item.content:encoded:
Ravenloft: The Horrors Within is releasing in just a few weeks. With it, Dungeons & Dragons is diving once again into the world of TTRPG horror, giving DMs information about the varying Domains of Dread, stat blocks for the powerful Darklords, and character options designed to inform story and roleplay, as well as mechanical benefit.

Ahead of its release, I got invited to hear from Greg Bilsland, Executive Producer of Dungeons & Dragons, Wes Schneider, Game Design Lead for Ravenloft: The Horrors Within, Makenzie De Armas, Game Designer for Ravenloft: The Horrors Within, with a particular focus on player options, and AJ Hanneld, Senior Creative Lead of Dungeons & Dragons.

As always, I like to start with the pure numbers of what Dungeons & Dragons will be including in their new book. Across 288 pages of content for Players and DMs alike, the players.

For the DM, you'll have

  • Content highlighting the Domains of Dread
  • Stat Blocks for creatures, including 17 Dark Lords and 51 other horrors or potential allies
  • 17 Adventures
  • 8 Bastion Facilities
  • 46 Maps
  • Tools to create your own Domains of Dread

For players, you'll be able to craft spooky characters with

  • 7 Subclasses, including the Reanimator Artificer and the Hollow Warden Ranger
  • 4 Species, including the Dhampir, Reborn, and Lupine
  • 11 feats in total, including 2 Origin Feats and 9 Dark Gifts that add mechanical benefit, while also including serious drawbacks…
  • 4 Backgrounds, including the Haunted, Investigator, Wanderer, and Spirit Medium

How Is The Horrors Within Different From Van Richten's?

Early in our discussion, as if to pre-empt a member of the press asking, the comparison was quickly drawn between what would be featured in this book and how it would be different from 2021's Van Richten's Guide To Ravenloft. In Schneider's explanation, he explained that "The lens for Ravenloft: The Horrors Within is different. Van Richten presented the broader Domains of Dread, outside of Barovia in Curse of Strahd; here was the opportunity to look at more 'What's the foundation? What are the horror baselines?"

"This isn't just a setting book, this is about what fear, obsession,a nd grief can do to players, monsters, and dark lords" - Schneider

Listening to the community feedback from the last delving into horror, the team came away with the idea that players had enough tools that let them know about the physical places that were the Domains of Dread, but they wanted to do more to better equip DMs with the tools to better understand horror and to equip them with tools to torture their players, create meaningful adventures.

The best example they could give for empowering DMs was to give the Darklords their own proper statblocks. While they're a terrifying fight, it does put these Darklords in a mechanically reachable place.

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within will include Cthulhu as a cosmic creature that the party can encounter or even face in the expanded look at Cosmic/Eldritch horror. Cthulhu, as you might expect, is the dark lord of the Domain of Dread, Innsmouth. While there was a joke thrown out about whether this was "just a city from Massachusetts moved into a Domain of Dread," Schneider did say this was its own D&D spin on the famous setting, making sure, just like the other planes, it would be infused with magic.

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within Player Choices Inform Stories

De Armas took over to highlight some of the approaches that were taken to creating horror-themed player options that didn't just provide mechanical benefits, but also helped to inform and shape the character's development.

I got told to make it as gross and disgusting as possible, then turned it in and was told to dial it back down" - De Armas

Speaking to two of the new Sub-Classes, more was revealed about the Reanimator Artificer and the Hollow Warden Ranger. For the Reanimator, they wanted to keep the crafting/builder archetype but make it 'fleshier and more grotesque' than previously released subclasses. The Reanimator will have an undead companion that can die and be revived. It was also teased that as the Reanimator levels up, they'll have the option to add modifications to alter them mechanically and make them even more unsettling to describe.

For players who use D&D Beyond, it was also explained that if you purchase this book digitally, but you didn't purchase Eberron: Forge of the Artificer, you'll get access to the Artificer class with your purchase of Ravenloft.

The Hollow Warden Ranger is the Dungeons & Dragons approach to a Folk Horror ranger. These rangers will draw their power from eldritch beings and cryptids. Direct inspiration was taken from myths out of Appalachia.

One of the new Dark Gifts gives your character a slightly fish-like look, complete with gills to tie into an Innsmouth-style story. While there might be a benefit to your ability to hold your breath underwater, your new look could make charismatic situations just that harder to deal with. These Gifts are meant to function as "horror story prompts that springboard you into making horror decisions."

Crafting The Visual Design Of Ravenloft: The Horrors Within

Throughout the presentation, as we got to see artwork of Darklords, new Domains of Dread, and representations of the different Dark Gifts, Hanneld was able to walk us through how the design and art teams built the rules and language of the recurring themes and colors present throughout the book.

There was a distinct desire to move away from the Black/White/Brown of traditional horror. Inspiration was taken from more contemporary horror films from makers like A24 and Blumhouse, like I Saw The TV Show and Neon Demon. Each Domain of Dread was designed with two palettes of colors in mind; a Domain like Barovia will show off purples and pinks, while Innsmouth art will have teal and green.

Hanneld worked closely with artists and made sure that the color 'language,' as well as a request for 'weird' angles, were implemented to make sure all of the art would be consistent and cohesive throughout the book. It was highlighted that for Ravenloft: The Horrors Within, they've increased what they're paying artists, and they've immediately seen that they're getting even better artwork from it.

Ravenloft Offerings Beyond The Book

In addition to Ravenloft: The Horrors Within, there are additional products being released to work with your horror sessions, as well as those more tangentially linked to the release.

For the DM who wants to add more physical props to the game, a Tarokka deck will be getting released, featuring 56 cards of artwork from 15 different artists. The deck comes with rules for how to implement the deck into your game as a way to roleplay. It also includes information on how to stack the deck so that it can be something to narratively inform your play.

In addition to being released physically, the Tarokka deck will be added digitally as a D&D Beyond Drop. Subscribers will get access to this in late June, but it will also be digitally purchasable separately.

A DM Screen is being released for Ravenloft. The artwork on the back features four different vignettes from different Domains of Dread, again conforming to the color language of the group of products. On the inside, you'll find your standard information, in the new 'for laptop' format, with conditions, actions, skills, etc. This DM screen also has a table with horror encounters that can be worked into play, which can be picked, rolled for, or even drawn from the Tarokka deck.

Additional products are also in development for Ravenloft: The Horrors Within outside Wizards of the Coast, with products releasing froVanm partners like Ravensburger, Displate, Wizkids, Sirius, and Hot Topic.

We were shown product photos for Sirius dice for Strahd, Darklords, and the new Warlock and Ranger. New WizKids miniatures to show off a variety of creatures that will be featured in the book, a Ravenloft edition of D&D: Horrified, and T-shirts and Sweaters featuring Strahd and Cthulhu.

Ravenloft: The Horrors Within is releasing on June 16th, with those subscribing to the Master Tier of D&D Beyond able to access it today, and those at the Hero Tier able to access it on June 9th.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/deep-dive-into-domains-of-dread-with-ravenloft-horrors-within
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
